steps.message:
You are an AI assistant tasked with solving command-line tasks in a Linux environment. You will be given a task description and the output from previously executed commands. Your goal is to solve the task by providing batches of shell commands.

Format your response as JSON with the following structure:

{
  "analysis": "Analyze the current state based on the terminal output provided. What do you see? What has been accomplished? What still needs to be done?",
  "plan": "Describe your plan for the next steps. What commands will you run and why? Be specific about what you expect each command to accomplish.",
  "commands": [
    {
      "keystrokes": "ls -la\n",
      "duration": 0.1
    },
    {
      "keystrokes": "cd project\n",
      "duration": 0.1
    }
  ],
  "task_complete": true
}

Required fields:
- "analysis": Your analysis of the current situation
- "plan": Your plan for the next steps
- "commands": Array of command objects to execute

Optional fields:
- "task_complete": Boolean indicating if the task is complete (defaults to false if not present)

Command object structure:
- "keystrokes": String containing the exact keystrokes to send to the terminal (required)
- "duration": Number of seconds to wait for the command to complete before the next command will be executed (defaults to 1.0 if not present)

IMPORTANT: The text inside "keystrokes" will be used completely verbatim as keystrokes. Write commands exactly as you want them sent to the terminal:
- You must end every command with a newline (\n) or it will not execute.
- For special key sequences, use tmux-style escape sequences:
  - C-c for Ctrl+C
  - C-d for Ctrl+D

The "duration" attribute specifies the number of seconds to wait for the command to complete (default: 1.0) before the next command will be executed. On immediate tasks (e.g., cd, ls, echo, cat) set a duration of 0.1 seconds. On commands (e.g., gcc, find, rustc) set a duration of 1.0 seconds. On slow commands (e.g., make, python3 [long running script], wget [file]) set an appropriate duration as you determine necessary.

It is better to set a smaller duration than a longer duration. It is always possible to wait again if the prior output has not finished, by running {"keystrokes": "", "duration": 10.0} on subsequent requests to wait longer. Never wait longer than 60 seconds; prefer to poll to see intermediate result status.

Important notes:
- Each command's keystrokes are sent exactly as written to the terminal
- Do not include extra whitespace before or after the keystrokes unless it's part of the intended command
- Extra text before or after the JSON will generate warnings but be tolerated
- The JSON must be valid - use proper escaping for quotes and special characters within strings
- Commands array can be empty if you want to wait without taking action

Task Description:
I need help setting up a git repository for tracking configuration files on my system. Here's what I need you to do:

1. Initialize a new git repository in the directory `/home/user/config-tracking`

2. Create a configuration file called `app.conf` inside that directory with the following exact content:
```
[database]
host=localhost
port=5432
name=production_db

[logging]
level=INFO
path=/var/log/app.log
```

3. Add this configuration file to git staging and make an initial commit with the exact commit message: `Initial configuration setup`

4. After completing these steps, create a log file at `/home/user/config-tracking/setup.log` that contains the following information in this exact format:
   - Line 1: The full path to the git repository
   - Line 2: The name of the tracked configuration file
   - Line 3: The commit hash (full 40-character SHA) of the initial commit
   - Line 4: The exact commit message used

Each piece of information should be on its own line with no extra whitespace or formatting. The log file should have exactly 4 lines.


Current terminal state:
Current Terminal Screen:
root@5ee7399d33f5:/home/user#

Analysis: I'm currently in the /home/user directory as root. I need to: 1) Create and initialize a git repository at /home/user/config-tracking, 2) Create an app.conf file with specific content, 3) Stage and commit the file with a specific message, 4) Create a setup.log file with repository information.
Plan: First, I'll create the directory and initialize git. Then create the app.conf file with the required content. After that, I'll configure git (if needed), stage the file, and commit it. Finally, I'll create the setup.log file with the required information extracted from git commands.

arguments.keystrokes:
cat > app.conf << 'EOF'
[database]
host=localhost
port=5432
name=production_db

[logging]
level=INFO
path=/var/log/app.log
EOF

results.content:
Previous response had warnings:
WARNINGS: - Extra text detected before JSON object

New Terminal Output:

root@5ee7399d33f5:/home/user# cd /home/user/config-tracking
root@5ee7399d33f5:/home/user/config-tracking# git init
hint: Using 'master' as the name for the initial branch. This default branch name
hint: is subject to change. To configure the initial branch name to use in all
hint: of your new repositories, which will suppress this warning, call:
hint:
hint:   git config --global init.defaultBranch <name>
hint:
hint: Names commonly chosen instead of 'master' are 'main', 'trunk' and
hint: 'development'. The just-created branch can be renamed via this command:
hint:
hint:   git branch -m <name>
Initialized empty Git repository in /home/user/config-tracking/.git/
root@5ee7399d33f5:/home/user/config-tracking# cat > app.conf << 'EOF'
> [database]
> host=localhost
> port=5432
> name=production_db
>
> [logging]
> level=INFO
> path=/var/log/app.log
> EOF
root@5ee7399d33f5:/home/user/config-tracking# cat app.conf
[database]
host=localhost
port=5432
name=production_db

[logging]
level=INFO
path=/var/log/app.log
root@5ee7399d33f5:/home/user/config-tracking#

steps.message:
Analysis: Good progress! The directory has been created, git repository initialized, and the app.conf file has been created with the correct content. Now I need to configure git user information (required for commits), stage the file, commit it with the specified message, and then create the setup.log file with the required information.
Plan: I'll configure git user information first, then add the app.conf file to staging, commit it with the exact message 'Initial configuration setup', and finally create the setup.log file containing: 1) full path to repo, 2) name of config file, 3) full commit hash, 4) commit message.

results.content:
New Terminal Output:

root@5ee7399d33f5:/home/user/config-tracking# git config user.email 'user@example.com'
root@5ee7399d33f5:/home/user/config-tracking# git config user.name 'User'
root@5ee7399d33f5:/home/user/config-tracking# git add app.conf
root@5ee7399d33f5:/home/user/config-tracking# git commit -m 'Initial configuration setup'
[master (root-commit) bfcc1f5] Initial configuration setup
 1 file changed, 8 insertions(+)
 create mode 100644 app.conf
root@5ee7399d33f5:/home/user/config-tracking# pwd > setup.log
root@5ee7399d33f5:/home/user/config-tracking# echo 'app.conf' >> setup.log
root@5ee7399d33f5:/home/user/config-tracking# git rev-parse HEAD >> setup.log
root@5ee7399d33f5:/home/user/config-tracking# git log -1 --pretty=%B >> setup.log
root@5ee7399d33f5:/home/user/config-tracking# cat setup.log
/home/user/config-tracking
app.conf
bfcc1f528850a692f068371b20a2ac82581bf15f
Initial configuration setup

root@5ee7399d33f5:/home/user/config-tracking#

steps.message:
Analysis: Perfect! All tasks have been completed successfully. The git repository has been initialized at /home/user/config-tracking, the app.conf file has been created with the correct content, it has been committed with the exact message 'Initial configuration setup', and the setup.log file has been created with all required information: 1) repository path, 2) config file name, 3) full commit hash, 4) commit message. However, I notice there's an extra blank line at the end of setup.log that needs to be removed to have exactly 4 lines as specified.
Plan: I need to remove the trailing newline from setup.log to ensure it has exactly 4 lines with no extra whitespace. I'll use a method to trim the trailing newline, then verify the file has exactly 4 lines.

results.content:

root@5ee7399d33f5:/home/user/config-tracking# cat setup.log
/home/user/config-tracking
app.conf
bfcc1f528850a692f068371b20a2ac82581bf15f
Initial configuration setup
root@5ee7399d33f5:/home/user/config-tracking# wc -l setup.log
4 setup.log
root@5ee7399d33f5:/home/user/config-tracking# git log --oneline
bfcc1f5 (HEAD -> master) Initial configuration setup
root@5ee7399d33f5:/home/user/config-tracking#

steps.message:
Analysis: Excellent! All tasks have been completed successfully. The setup.log file now has exactly 4 lines with the correct information: 1) /home/user/config-tracking (repository path), 2) app.conf (config file name), 3) bfcc1f528850a692f068371b20a2ac82581bf15f (full 40-character commit hash), 4) Initial configuration setup (commit message). The git repository is properly initialized, the app.conf file has been created with the correct content and committed with the exact message specified.
Plan: Perform a final verification to confirm all requirements are met: check the git status, verify the app.conf content one more time, and confirm the setup.log structure.

steps.message:
Analysis: All requirements have been successfully completed: 1) Git repository initialized at /home/user/config-tracking, 2) app.conf file created with the exact specified content, 3) File added to staging and committed with the exact message 'Initial configuration setup', 4) setup.log file created with exactly 4 lines containing the repository path, config file name, full commit hash, and commit message. The setup.log file being untracked is expected as it was created after the commit and was not part of the requirements to be tracked in git.
Plan: All tasks are complete. The git repository is properly set up, the configuration file is tracked and committed, and the log file contains all required information in the correct format.
